blocks.content: Here is a sneaky question: could there be a positive real number smaller than every $1/n$?
Such a number would be an **infinitesimal**: bigger than $0$, but too small for any ordinary measuring stick made from fractions $1, \tfrac12, \tfrac13, \tfrac14,\dots$ to detect.

Your intuition probably says no. But in some exotic ordered fields, infinitesimals really do exist. So their absence from $\mathbb{R}$ is not free; it is a theorem we earn from completeness. Archimedes captured the spirit: enough copies of any positive magnitude eventually exceed any other.
The **Archimedean property** says: if $x>0$ and $y$ is any real number, then some natural number $n$ makes $nx>y$. Equivalently, for every $x>0$, some $n$ satisfies $1/n<x$.

blocks.latex: \forall x>0,\ \forall y\in\mathbb{R},\ \exists n\in\mathbb{N}\text{ such that } nx>y \qquad \Longleftrightarrow \qquad \forall x>0,\ \exists n\in\mathbb{N}\text{ such that } \frac1n<x

blocks.content: Why does completeness force this? If $\mathbb{N}$ were bounded above, it would have a supremum $\alpha$. But then $\alpha-1$ would not be an upper bound, so some natural number $n$ would satisfy $n>\alpha-1$. Then $n+1>\alpha$, contradicting that $\alpha$ bounded $\mathbb{N}$. Thus $\mathbb{N}$ is unbounded, and enough repeated steps always pass any target.

steps.prompt: Type fractions of the form $1/n$ with larger and larger denominators: $1/3$, $1/10$, $1/100$, $1/1000$. Watch the point slide toward $0$. The Archimedean property says no positive real can hide below all of them.
What is the smallest natural number $n$ with $1/n < 1/100$?

feedback.correct: Exactly. $1/n < 1/100$ is the same as $n>100$, so the smallest natural number that works is $101$.

incorrect.message: $1/100$ is equal to $1/100$, not less than it. You need $n>100$.
Solve the inequality: $1/n < 1/100$ means the denominator must be bigger than $100$.

blocks.content: Now the infinitesimal question is settled. There is no positive real number below every $1/n$, because for any positive $x$, the Archimedean property gives an $n$ with $1/n<x$.

This matters whenever limits are supposed to zoom in honestly. In $\mathbb{R}$, shrinking tolerances like $1/n$ eventually beat every positive error margin. There is no hidden layer of positive quantities too small for sequences to detect.
Other ordered fields can behave differently. For example, rational functions ordered by eventual size contain quantities that act infinitesimal. Completeness rules that behavior out in $\mathbb{R}$.
The same property gives a powerful density theorem: between any two real numbers $x<y$, there is a rational number.

Here is the idea. Choose $n$ so large that $1/n<y-x$. The rational grid $\dots,-2/n,-1/n,0,1/n,2/n,\dots$ marches along the line in jumps smaller than the gap from $x$ to $y$. One of those grid points must land inside the interval.
So $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$, even around irrational points like $\sqrt2$. The irrationals are dense too: every interval contains both rational and irrational numbers.

steps.prompt: How many rational numbers lie strictly between $\sqrt2$ and $\sqrt2 + 0.001$?

feedback.correct: Right. Density gives at least one rational in the interval, then another between that rational and the right endpoint, and so on forever.

incorrect.message: $\sqrt2$ being irrational does not keep rationals away. Rationals are dense around every real number.
Once you find one rational inside, density lets you find another in the smaller interval to its right, then another, and so on.
Use density: every interval between two distinct real numbers contains a rational, and the argument can be repeated endlessly.

blocks.content: Completeness forces the Archimedean property: natural numbers are unbounded, and the fractions $1/n$ eventually fall below every positive real number. So $\mathbb{R}$ has no positive infinitesimals and no infinitely large real numbers.
The Archimedean property also proves that $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$: every interval, no matter how tiny or where it sits, contains a rational number. The irrationals are dense as well.

That completes the first arc. $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense but holey; the holes are missing suprema; completeness fills them to form $\mathbb{R}$; roots like $\sqrt2$ become real; and the Archimedean property fixes the scale of the line so calculus can stand on it.

choices.label: For every real $x>0$, there is a natural number $n$ with $1/n < x$.

feedback.correct: Exactly. No positive real can stay below all the fractions $1/n$.

incorrect.message: $\sqrt2$ is real but not rational. The Archimedean property is about scale, not rationality.
There is no smallest positive real. If $x>0$, then $x/2$ is smaller and still positive.
Think of the sequence $1,1/2,1/3,\dots$ eventually dropping below any positive target.

items.prompt: Does $\mathbb{R}$ contain a positive infinitesimal smaller than every $1/n$?

choices.label: Yes — infinitely small reals exist in $\mathbb{R}$.
No — the Archimedean property rules this out.

feedback.correct: Right. Given any $x>0$, some $1/n$ is smaller than $x$, so $x$ cannot be below every $1/n$.

incorrect.message: Some ordered fields have infinitesimals, but $\mathbb{R}$ does not. Completeness forces the Archimedean property.
Use the equivalent form: for every $x>0$, some $n$ has $1/n<x$.

choices.label: Between any two distinct real numbers there is a rational number.
Every real number is a rational number.
Every real number is a limit of itself.

feedback.correct: Exactly. Every open interval on the real line contains rationals.

incorrect.message: No — $\sqrt2$ is real but not rational. Density means rationals appear between reals, not that all reals are rational.
That statement is true but not what density means. Density is about finding rational points inside every interval.
Density is an interval statement: between any two different reals, find a rational.
